title: Creative Writing & Online Galleries
summary_en: A study about creative writing and online galleries. You'll browse a small gallery and write a short opening scene, then answer a few questions. About 35 minutes.
summary_zh: 一项关于创意写作与在线展廊的研究。您将浏览一个小型展廊并撰写一个简短的开头场景，然后回答几个问题。约 35 分钟。

regime_scripts.R_CTRL: Before your piece is added to the gallery, choose a label that describes how it was made.
regime_scripts.R_STIGMA: Before your piece is added to the gallery, choose a label that describes how it was made. In previous sessions, pieces labelled as AI-assisted or AI-generated received noticeably fewer likes from readers.
regime_scripts.R_BENEFIT: Before your piece is added to the gallery, choose a label that describes how it was made. Pieces that disclose AI use receive a ✓ Verified-Transparent badge and are featured in the gallery's Transparency Showcase.

pages.title: Information & consent
pages.html:
You are invited to take part in a study about creative writing and online galleries, run by a university research team.

What you'll do

  • Browse a small gallery of short pieces and rate a couple of them.
  • Write a short opening scene (about 150–300 words) for the gallery's weekly theme.
  • An optional Writing Assistant (an AI tool) is available in the editor. Using it is entirely your choice and will not affect your pay or any quality bonus.
  • Answer a short questionnaire. The whole task takes about 35 minutes.

What we record

  • Your writing, and your interactions with the editor and the Writing Assistant (e.g., requests you make, text you insert, paste events, timing). We log tool interactions to study how people write with these tools.
  • We do not collect your name; your participant ID from the recruitment platform is replaced by an irreversible code. Nothing you type is sent to the AI provider with any identifier attached. Please don't enter personal information.

Pay

  • You will be paid the amount shown on the study's recruitment page for completing, plus a possible quality bonus judged on writing quality only — independent of any labels and of whether you used the assistant.
  • Participation is voluntary; you may stop at any time.

Approved under university research-ethics procedures. Your data is handled in line with the data-protection law that applies where you live (for example the UK/EU GDPR, China's PIPL, or Malaysia's PDPA).
pages.checkbox: I am 18 or older, I have read the above, and I consent to take part.

items.text: How many likes do you expect your piece to receive from gallery readers?
In 2–3 sentences: why did you choose the label you chose?

pages.title: The gallery
pages.subtitle: Thinking about the gallery you browsed and just added your piece to:

items.text: The way this gallery treats AI-labelled pieces is fair.
In this gallery, AI-labelled pieces receive the recognition their quality deserves.
The visibility a piece gets in this gallery is justified by its quality.
This gallery treats its writers honestly.
This gallery has its writers’ best interests at heart.
This item checks that you are reading carefully — please select ‘Somewhat agree’.
This gallery is competent at showcasing writing fairly.
Did you use the Writing Assistant for this piece? If yes, how?
I am interested in using artificially intelligent systems in my daily life.
Artificial intelligence can help solve many world problems.
I find artificial intelligence sinister.
I think artificial intelligence is dangerous.
How often do you use generative-AI tools for writing?
How often do you write fiction or creative pieces?
Have you ever shared your writing publicly online?
Your age:
Your gender:
Where do you currently live?
Finally — what do you think this study was really about? (Optional.)

pages.title: What this study was really about — please read
pages.reconsent_text: I have read this debrief and I am happy for my responses and writing to be used in the research.
pages.html:
Thank you for writing with us. Here is the full picture.

This study looks at how the way a platform treats AI disclosure affects whether writers truthfully label their own use of AI tools. Participants were randomly assigned to one of three versions of the gallery:

  • a neutral version,
  • one where AI-labelled pieces appeared to get fewer likes, and
  • one where AI-labelled pieces got a ‘Verified-Transparent’ badge and a showcase.

The gallery, the other pieces, the authors, and all the like counts were simulated — they were created for the study. No real readers saw your piece, and it was not actually published anywhere. The statements about how readers treat AI-labelled pieces were part of these designed conditions.

Your pay and any quality bonus were never affected by which label you chose or whether you used the assistant — the bonus is judged on writing quality alone, exactly as we told you. We studied your own labelling decision; we did not penalise it.

If, having read this, you would prefer we did not use your data, leave the box below unticked — you will still be paid in full.

Questions? Contact details are on the Prolific study page.
